speaker: interviewer
text: --N-C-B-A-U-M. This is roll number three.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the end of 1944. And you were on the train to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when you got to Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got to Bergen-Belsen, we didn't know what this is. We saw burning-- ovens were burning. We didn't know-- we-- alive-- alive people burning or dead people. We didn't know. They put it in a-- in a barrack, on the floor. You didn't have nothing-- no pillow, no cover, nothing. And we slept on the floor. We ate on the floor. They gave us very little to eat. And-- but we were clean. But after a few weeks, the Romanian people came-- Hungarian. And there were lice. Like, you-- you couldn't be clean. We had lice. You could have put in a hand and-- and grab a-- a full-- a full hand, a full fist. Because they didn't wash themself. They didn't clean themself. And it was just awful, just awful. When I heard that transports are coming to Bergen-Belsen, first my girlfriend-- what she is now, she was like a sister to me-- she was looking for me. She heard that there's a transport coming. So she found me. She was there before already. And she worked as a helper in a barrack, to a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So she came to me. We were very close before. And she said to me, I'll take you in my barrack to sleep. You get a bed. So I said, I can't go with you. Because I met my sister-in-law also-- my brother's wife-- in Bergen-Belsen. So I said, I can't leave her. So she said, I'll take you both. You'll sleep in one bed. I had to get up very, very early, to come to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the counting. And it wasn't too good, either. Because people saw what they lived-- they-- it wasn't for long. I stayed in my-- back-- I went back to my place, my barrack. What was the barrack like in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad. I told. You we slept on the floor. And we-- you couldn't stretch, one on top of other. And I met a girlfriend from home. She is in France. I have even a picture of her. And I tried to help myself. And I feed them too-- my sister-in-law and my-- and my friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I do that? I worked. I-- I bought-- I took-- I took by people what [? there ?] [? weren't ?] in Auschwitz. They had dollars. They had gold. And I sold to a [NON-ENGLISH], to a-- what-- she was [? hatter ?] for us. What she had bread, and then-- and margarine, and jam, and soups. She-- she gave us all that to eat. So I sold-- I gave it for some-- for a pail of soup. So I had left over a little bit soup, a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told my sister-in-law, you sit and watch the bread. Because there was stealing. There were fathers and sons, or mothers and daughters, and sisters and sisters. They were-- they didn't say, take a piece of bread. They were saying-- they put their hands on there. They-- which one you want, this bigger, or this bigger? But I wasn't like that. I wasn't like that. So an SS man came in the barrack. And he says, who wants to go to work and get a bed to sleep? I raised my hand. I was healthy. If I went, my sister-in-law went with me. And my friend couldn't go, because she was sick already. She was laying on the floor. She couldn't go. This was the biggest mistake, what I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: They made-- we were practically without shoes, in the mud. It was late, and rain, and mud. We were carrying trees on our shoulders. And it was awful-- awful. And the dogs were running after us. And they gave us a bed, a military bed-- the small, narrow ones. And four girls had to sleep, two in one side, and two in the other side. It was worse than the floor-- much worse.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was so awful. My sister-in-law was sick. And she couldn't go-- she couldn't go back. I didn't know what to do. We-- I was trying-- I-- I was talking to my friend. Can you do something for me? She said, she'll try, because oh, the lady was on top of her-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH]. That's was it called. She maybe could do something. So I had a watch left over, what was-- what I was selling-- gold, and silver, and everything. So I said, tell her I'll give her the watch, the gold watch. And she should take us out. Well, this was already late in Bergen-Belsen-- late, because after that, we were liberated. But we went through a lot, a lot. Tell me just some of the things that you went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I say, we-- I couldn't walk almost already. Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you don't-- when it's raining, and it's muddy, and it's cold, you carry trees on your shoulder-- one on one shoulder, the other girl on the other shoulder. And walking maybe a half a mile, and the dog runs after you, you can't stop. We wanted to die already. It was unbelievable. We couldn't take it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you keep going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't-- really don't know what kept me going. Maybe-- maybe I'll find somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The British--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --liberated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the day, the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was April the 15th, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- they took us out from those filthy barracks. And they sent us to depot. First of all, 30,000 people died the first week after the liberation. Because they-- they meant well. They cooked for us rice with fat-- so much fat. Our stomachs were empty. And the fat made the dysentery, the sickness. And 30,000 people died in one week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back just a little bit. What was it like for you when you first saw the British?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was wonderful. When-- when I got-- my girlfriend spoke English. So she could talk a little bit to them. And we-- it was more, a little bit, food. I-- I really didn't eat the soup what they gave me. If I had some, I took off all the fat. We went-- that time, it was three days. You could have take anything from the German. Steal-- not steal, take it-- money, clothes, other things, anything you wanted. But we didn't go. We just went to the field. And we dig some potatoes. And we had a friend, and man. He brought some-- he went into a German family. He said, give me some eggs. Give me a chicken. And we cooked, ourselves. He was the-- the cook. And we slept-- we were six girls in one room. But they took us out from those barracks. And they sent us depots for three months. And they wanted to clean up the barracks, the-- the homes-- big buildings, where the SS used to live. And they brought us back later, after three months. My sister-in-law got sick. And she had water in-- in the side. She laid maybe three months, and-- or two months in the hospital-- more--

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were liberated--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: --did the-- the guards just run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they left you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left, yes. Some run away. Some-- some said there was a [NON-ENGLISH]. They were arrested. Some were arrested. We just had nobody should watch us. We were like, free-- completely free. But we-- we didn't know where to go. We didn't know where to go. After a while-- we lived in-- in barracks. My brother was in Buchenwald. And he was with my brother-in-law, from Warsaw. He met my brother-in-law in-- in Buchenwald. And they were together. And the last minute, my brother said, when they took us-- on a march, they took them. So my brother said to him, let's hide. So he said, I can't hide. Where will I hide? So he went on the march. And my brother took away a piece of wood from the floor, from the barrack. And he went underneath. And he stayed there for a few days, until he heard already that the American are there. And some workers-- Jews, but they still cleaned up-- knew that he's there. An uncle knew he's there. So he brought him in some food. When he went out, they-- they were sending lists all over Germany, to all kind of camps, who is-- where somebody is alive. So all of a sudden, he saw Paula Lehrman and [? Pesa ?] Lehrman. He didn't know what to do-- my name and his wife's name. So he went to a German couple. He said, you have-- give me a bicycle. They had to give him. He gave him a bicycle. He came to Bergen-Belsen on a bicycle. Took him a few days. And when he came, we got-- everybody had a-- they gave you an apartment-- an apartment-- a room, you know, to live. So after a while, when he came, he wanted I should stay with him. So I stayed with him. And when sister-in-law was sick. So it wasn't a picnic. I cooked. And I cleaned. And I brought her food. And later, she got well. And she was pregnant. And she gave birth in Bergen-Belsen, to a little boy. Aaron-- his name. He was a very smart kid. But, by a year and a half, like I said-- yeah, and I-- and he opened a store, like a candy store, my brother. And I was selling the ice cream, and fruits, and vegetables. And I went to-- from Bergen-Belsen to Munich. I was smuggling, to make some money. And I brought back Sweet'N Low, like they-- the round ones, the saccharine. And we had to have already a few dollars. And I met my husband there, in Bergen-Belsen. And he was also smuggling from-- he smuggled in already big trucks with coffee, and cigarettes, diamonds from Berlin. I met him. And we got married in January 28, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got married in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen, yes. And there was a community, like, in Bergen-Belsen. They had a shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how-- about how many people were living there then?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people came from-- from Lodz, from other camps, to Bergen-Belsen, to look for people. Of course, I don't know-- I don't know exactly how many people was there, no. But it was maybe-- buildings was maybe [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 50 buildings. And everybody had a-- was a family-- sisters, brothers, friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a school there, and/or a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They made themself a synagogue. There was a school, yes. They were teaching children. It was a committee from-- all the Jews. They made a committee. And it was the Israeli soldiers-- a few, what they came, with the British. And they were help, of course. They brought clothes. And they brought food. And we tried to live a little bit there. And I gave birth the same year, October 26, 1948, to a little girl, Shirley. In Germany, I called her Sarah. But in the States, we-- I changed it. And my nephew, my brother's child, after a few-- he was a year and a half. He was so smart. We locked ourself up-- this is just a funny story. And we didn't have-- we locked the door. We couldn't get in. And he was by himself. He was a year and a half. And we start talking to him-- Aaron, Aaron, take a little chair, a step stool. Go to the window. Look for the keys. He was so smart. And he-- and he will throw it out through the window. It took a while. But he did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But later, he got sick. Because he caught the-- from a cousin-- how do you call it? I forgot how you call it-- pneumonia, he had. And they took him to Switzerland to cure him. And he was there four years. While you were in Bergen-Belsen, and after you had gotten married, did you and your husband discuss where you wanted to go eventually?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we wanted to go to Israel. My hus-- we had a friend in Israel. He went before us. He-- we-- he had sent away a factory for makes those blocks, to build homes. He went to a town in Germany. And A bought, for $48,000-- we had money at that time, already-- those machineries what makes the cent and everything. And he-- he took some lessons. And we sent it away to Germany-- to Israel. And I sent away clothes. And I sent away a lot of stuff to Ger-- to Israel. Because we wanted to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this guy, [? Zilberschwartz, ?] [? Haim ?] [? Zilberschwartz, ?] with his wife, [? Thela, ?] she wrote us a letter. And she said-- no, this was already, in-- yeah, in Bergen-Belsen. She said, don't come. It's very bad. Go to the United States. People were dying that time, too. There was a war. So we left us everything. We started to try to go to United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother came four weeks before me to the States. He lived in a furnished room. I supposed to go-- we supposed to go to Detroit, Michigan. But I had one brother left. So I wanted to stay with him. So they let me. And we didn't know where to go. So he took us in, with permission from the landlady. In his furnished room, a week we slept four in a bed. And my baby was 14 months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949, December 24. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In 1949, December 24th, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the trip to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very sick. I was throwing up. And my husband-- three days he took-- he couldn't take of the baby, either. She was laying 24 hours in one diaper, dirty. After three-- he-- he had a [NON-ENGLISH] on his hand, a watch. A [NON-ENGLISH] is-- he wanted to give a steward this, this watch-- she should change the baby. So she changed once. But she didn't want any watch, no. I didn't see my ship. When we came to this States-- so my husband said, and a friend, come out, you'll see the ship. I was still sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to what city in the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to New York. Yes. General Blatchford was my ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Germany--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived for a week with my brother. And I bought a carriage for my baby, she should sleep, because we couldn't [INAUDIBLE]. After that, my husband met [INAUDIBLE] in the Bronx. And I lived in-- first of all, no. I had a-- I rented a furnished room in Brownsville, on the third floor. I had to schlep down the-- the carriage, every day. But she was very good to us, very good. I made [NON-ENGLISH]. She loved my baby. She-- she never ate by herself. She had to sit on her lap. Even, she put her fingers in the food. She wasn't-- she ate it. When we wanted to move out, a [? landsman ?] from my-- from my husband want-- he gave us an apartment, in a whole building. We had to pay $500, you know, under the table. So she said, maybe you don't have money to pay me. I'll keep you without money. She was very good to us. Once [LAUGHS] I was sick. I was laying. I took a nap in a-- in the furnished room, when I lived. And a bone came out from my wrist. So they told us to go to Sutton-- Sutton Avenue-- Sutton Place-- I don't know-- to a hospital. So I went there, to the hospital. And the doctor gave me a shot in the bone. And I was very observant. I looked around. And it came in my mind a telephone I saw on the wall. When I came home the same day, during the night, I was so sick. I thought, I'm gonna die. I had such pains from it. My landlady wasn't home. She went-- it was Friday night. She went to her son. And so I said, we have to call-- you know, [? Haimi, ?] I remember a telephone I have in my head, what I saw there. And I called it. I couldn't speak English. I said, come. Give a shot. I-- I couldn't speak English. This was an emergency number, for heart attacks, and other things.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were running over the streets with fire engines. And they rang the bell, on the third floor. When they walked in, I didn't know this is oxygen. I knew my aunt had the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she was selling sodas and ice cream. So this was like for sodas to make. So I didn't get scared. I-- I thought it a-- something for water. When I-- when they saw me-- who's sick? And I said, I am the sick one. You the sick one? You called it. Where did you get this number? I said, I had in my mind. I have-- I was very sick. So we waked-- I woke up a neighbor. And she explained. If I wouldn't be a newcomer, I would be very punished at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, we moved in an apartment. I had a very nice apartment-- one bedroom. But I had a living room, my eat-in kitchen, and a foyer-- comfortable. We came with a little bit money. So matter of fact, those things what I have here in the closet, we shipped in boxes. We could have take everything. Oh. And I-- I don't know [INAUDIBLE].
